svg.font-family: -apple-system, Segoe UI, Roboto, Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif
svg.aria-label: What the June 26 Lutnick license exception resolves: the EAR-vs-IEEPA legal-basis question, and the shape of the resolution.
svg.title: The Jun-26 partial lift is a BIS license exception (Annex A) — which both answers the Jun-25 EAR-vs-IEEPA question and shows the resolution arriving by a scoped-roster door, not the predicted Jul-8 Persona ID path.
svg.text: What the Jun-26 license exception tells us
The mechanism of the partial lift — a no-license exception keyed to Annex A — is itself evidence.
Jun-26 Lutnick letter: Mythos 5 may be exported WITHOUT a license to entities on Annex A
(“Anthropic US Entities — Approved,” ~100 orgs) + their foreign-national staff + US civilian agencies & national labs.
A “license exception” is pure EAR machinery. You only
grant a license exception to something that is an
“export subject to the EAR.” So the order is being run
on the EAR path — not IEEPA emergency authority.
A scoped, verified-access resolution — as predicted.
But it arrived via an Annex A ROSTER (who you are),
not the Jul-8 Persona ID path (prove where you are).
The model cleared FIRST is Mythos 5 — the classifier-stripped, safeguard-free build — routed to ~100 vetted
cyber defenders. The guarded, mass-market Fable 5 (classifiers route risky queries to Opus 4.8) stays dark worldwide.
Not a court ruling. The order's full text remains unpublished; the EAR read is inferred from the exception's form, corroborated
by CNN / CNBC / Semafor / NBC / Fortune reporting on the Jun-26 letter. Architecture (shared weights, classifier gate): Jun-11 §2, Jun-13.
